did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an optimized deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture, specifically DenseNet169, with custom mini-batch logic and a custom loss function to classify dermoscopic images for melanoma diagnosis. The AI model's performance was evaluated and compared against 157 dermatologists on the MClass-D dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of an optimized deep CNN architecture (DenseNet169) with custom loss functions and mini-batch logic. It reports new results from training on the ISIC 2019 dataset and evaluating performance against 157 dermatologists on the MClass-D dataset, including specific metrics like AUC, sensitivity, and specificity.

title: AI outperformed every dermatologist in dermoscopic melanoma diagnosis, using an optimized deep‑CNN architecture with custom mini‑batch logic and loss function
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of imbalanced datasets in dermoscopic melanoma diagnosis by proposing an optimized deep CNN architecture based on DenseNet169. The authors introduced custom mini-batch logic and a custom loss function to improve sensitivity and specificity balance during training on the ISIC 2019 dataset. The model's performance was evaluated on the MClass-D dataset, which included 100 dermoscopic images and diagnostic results from 157 dermatologists. The AI system achieved an area under the curve (AUC) of 94.4%, with a sensitivity of 85.0% and specificity of 95.0% at a standard threshold. These results demonstrate that the proposed AI model outperformed every one of the 157 dermatologists involved in the benchmark study.

lesion_types_studied.details: The minority class in the binary classification task. Used in the ISIC 2019 dataset (4,503 images in the study subset) and the MClass-D dataset (20 images). The study focuses on predicting melanoma.

The majority class in the binary classification task. Used in the ISIC 2019 dataset (12,799 images in the study subset) and the MClass-D dataset (80 images). Referred to as 'non-melanoma' in the context of the binary classification problem.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into melanoma (positive) or nevus/non-melanoma (negative) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'melanoma classification problem' and 'binary melanoma classification'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. nevus) using a sigmoid activation function for binary classification. Performance is evaluated using AUC, sensitivity, and specificity, which are metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public skin lesion image dataset from the ISIC 2019 challenge, containing dermoscopic images across eight categories. The study used a subset of images classified as melanoma and nevus, excluding images present in the MClass-D benchmark to avoid data leakage.

use.training: Used for training the optimized deep CNN architecture (DenseNet169) with custom mini-batch logic and loss function.
use.validation: Used for selecting the final model with minimal loss during training.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of the trained models (Test-10 dataset).

datasets_used.name: MClass-D
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset released by Brinker et al., consisting of 100 dermoscopic images and diagnostic results from 157 dermatologists from 12 university hospitals in Germany. The images were selected from the ISIC archive but were removed from the ISIC 2019 training/testing splits.

use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation of the AI models and for comparison against the diagnostic performance of 157 dermatologists.
use.other: Used as a benchmark for comparing AI performance against human dermatologists.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the diagnostic performance of the AI models and 157 dermatologists on the MClass-D dataset.
To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the AI models and 157 dermatologists, specifically measuring the true positive rate.
To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the AI models and 157 dermatologists, specifically measuring the true negative rate.
To visualize the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity across different thresholds for the AI models and compare them against dermatologists.
Used as a loss function component (lMSE, lPMSE, lNMSE) to optimize model weights during training.
Designed to balance sensitivity and specificity by optimizing the sum and difference of positive and negative mean squared errors during training on imbalanced data.
To select the final model architecture and weights during the training process, ensuring the model with the minimal loss on the validation dataset was chosen for evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the AI model and dermatologists were made using descriptive statistics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity) without statistical significance testing.

Test-10 dataset (ISIC 2019 subset).description: Evaluation of models ORI, BON, and BLF on the Test-10 dataset (10% of ISIC 2019) using a prediction threshold of 0.5.
Test-10 dataset (ISIC 2019 subset).notes: BLF provided the most balanced SEN and SPE. At SEN 85.0%, BLF achieved SPE of 89.9%, ORI 88.6%, and BON 88.8%. BLF outperformed InceptionV3 methods using Binary Cross-Entropy loss.

MClass-D dataset.description: Evaluation of models ORI, BON, and BLF on the MClass-D dataset (100 images) and comparison against 157 dermatologists. Threshold 0.5 used for primary metrics; other thresholds analyzed for balance.
MClass-D dataset.notes: BLF outperformed every one of the 157 dermatologists. 154 dermatologists' performance points were below the BLF ROC curve. BLF achieved a new state-of-the-art on the MClass-D dataset. BLF's AUC was 4.0% higher than Young et al. and 8.3% higher than InceptionV3 methods using Binary Cross-Entropy loss.

architecture_details.modified_layers: Reformed fully connected layers with two hidden layers using ReLU activation. First hidden layer has 1024 nodes, second has 512 nodes. Both hidden layers use Batch Normalization and Dropout (rate 0.5).
architecture_details.output_layer: Sigmoid activation function for binary classification
architecture_details.regularization: Dropout (rate 0.5) after each hidden layer; Batch Normalization in each hidden layer

optimizer_params.learning_rate: 0.0001 (base), with Cyclical Learning Rate (CLR) ranging from 0.0000001 to 0.0001

training_details.loss_function: Custom Loss Function (CLF) combining Mean Squared Error (MSE), Positive MSE (lPMSE), and Negative MSE (lNMSE) to balance sensitivity and specificity
training_details.mini_batch_logic: Custom mini-batch logic ensuring equal proportion of melanoma and nevus classes in every iteration
training_details.model_selection: Model with minimal validation loss (val_loss) selected

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: ORI (Original DenseNet169 with standard mini-batch and loss)
BON (DenseNet169 with custom mini-batch logic and standard loss)
BLF (DenseNet169 with custom mini-batch logic and custom loss function)
InceptionV3 with Mean Square Error loss
preprocessing_techniques: Center cropping of high-resolution images (up to 1800 × 1200 pixels)
Normalization by adding multiples to convert all pixels into the [0, 1.0] range
Image augmentation including: rotation within 5°, shifting within 0.05% amplitude, shear within 0.05°, zoom in/out within 0.05%, and fill mode using 'nearest' algorithm

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to enhance the effect of the custom loss function. Transforms included: (1) Image rotation within 5°; (2) Simultaneous image shifting within 0.05% amplitude (right/left and up/down); (3) Image shear within 0.05°; (4) Image zoom in and out within 0.05%; (5) Fill mode using the 'nearest' algorithm.

Ensured the proportion of melanoma (minority) and nevus (majority) classes remained fixed and unchanged in every mini-batch of training, rather than relying on random selection which could lead to dynamic and imbalanced class distributions.

conclusions: The proposed AI method, utilizing an optimized deep CNN architecture (DenseNet169) with custom mini-batch logic and a custom loss function, outperformed every one of the 157 dermatologists in dermoscopic melanoma diagnosis on the MClass-D dataset.
The AI model achieved an area under the curve (AUC) of 94.4%, a sensitivity of 85.0%, and a specificity of 95.0% at a standard threshold of 0.5.
Using the best threshold, the model demonstrated a highly balanced performance with a sensitivity of 90.0% and a specificity of 93.8%, which was more balanced compared to other researches.

The custom loss function and custom mini-batch logic effectively addressed the challenge of imbalanced datasets, improving the balance between sensitivity and specificity compared to standard approaches.
The proposed approach achieved higher performance than the state-of-the-art approach and previous studies, establishing a new benchmark for melanoma diagnosis.

The study acknowledges that training neural networks on imbalanced datasets typically leads to imbalanced performance (high specificity but low sensitivity), which the proposed method successfully mitigated.
The authors made the source code and data splits of all experiments publicly available to foster further research and allow for replicability.
important_remarks: The study lacks formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to confirm the significance of the performance difference between the AI model and dermatologists.
The evaluation dataset (MClass-D) is small (n=100 images), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the benchmark comparison.

The dermatologist cohort is geographically biased, consisting exclusively of 157 dermatologists from 12 university hospitals in Germany, limiting external validity to other populations or healthcare settings.

The AI model was trained on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which contains images from multiple sources and potentially different acquisition devices, introducing potential domain shift when evaluated on the specific MClass-D subset.

The claim of outperforming 'every' dermatologist relies on a specific subset of the original Brinker et al. data; the paper notes that previous state-of-the-art models only outperformed 136 of the 157 dermatologists, highlighting the sensitivity of the benchmark to specific image subsets or thresholds.

The study addresses class imbalance through algorithmic modifications (custom loss and mini-batch logic) rather than data-level resampling, which may not fully reflect real-world prevalence rates without further calibration.

No external validation was performed on an independent dataset separate from the ISIC archive and MClass-D, raising concerns about overfitting to the specific distribution of dermoscopic images in these public repositories.
